original_abstract: Current top performing object detectors employ detection proposals to guide the search for objects, thereby avoiding exhaustive sliding window search across images. Despite the popularity and widespread use of detection proposals, it is unclear which trade-offs are made when using them during object detection. We provide an in-depth analysis of twelve proposal methods along with four baselines regarding proposal repeatability, ground truth annotation recall on PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO, and their impact on DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN detection performance. Our analysis shows that for object detection improving proposal localisation accuracy is as important as improving recall. We introduce a novel metric, the average recall (AR), which rewards both high recall and good localisation and correlates surprisingly well with detection performance. Our findings show common strengths and weaknesses of existing methods, and provide insights and metrics for selecting and tuning proposal methods.
humanized_abstract: Detection proposal methods have become the de facto standard for object detection. We investigate the design decisions that are made using these proposal methods and their relative merits. We analyze the performance of 12 popular region proposal methods on 4 datasets along with 4 baseline methods. We analyze the repeatability, ground truth coverage, and the performance of Detection Proposal Network (DPM), R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN. Our results show that for object detection, improving localization accuracy of the proposal is as important as improving the recall of the proposals. We also introduce a novel metric, average recall (AR), to measure the balance between recall and localization accuracy of the proposals, and surprisingly, correlates highly with the accuracy of object detection models. Overall, our work gives an overview of the strengths and weaknesses of the widely used region proposal methods, as well as the metrics to use when choosing or designing proposal methods.
